coredata.dc:title: Black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals fabricated through square-wave alternating voltage as high-performance anode materials for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yingchang
Shi, Wei
Liao, Shijia
Zhang, Renhui
Leng, Senlin
coredata.dc:description: Black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals have been successfully fabricated via electrochemical square-wave alternating voltage method. Repeated redox of Ti foil surface accompanying vigorous hydrogen evolution plays a key role on the formation of the black TiO2 nanocrystals. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area are utilized to characterize the structure and properties of the products. When utilized as anode materials for lithium-ion batteries, the black TiO2 nanocrystals electrode exhibits a high reversible capacity of 271 and 266 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (1 C = 168 mA g−1), as well as a good rate capability, which are likely to be associated with small particle size (ca. 6 nm), surface/interfacial pseudocapacitive lithium storage and increased electronic conductivity contributed from Ti3+ defects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black TiO2
Lithium-ion battery
Alternating voltage
Energy storage
Electrochemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial supports from Department of Education of Guizhou Province (No. KY[2016]106 , KY[2016]009 ), Department of Science and Technology of Guizhou Province ( QKH[2017]1185 ) and Natural Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Guizhou Province ( QKH [2017] 5604 ) are greatly appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of B-TiO2 NCs, W-TiO2 NCs, TiO2 NPs, and the JCPDS standard card for anatase TiO2.
TEM (a, c, e) and HRTEM (b, d, f) images of TiO2 NPs (a, b), W-TiO2 NCs (c, d) and B-TiO2 NCs (e, f).
The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (a, b) and the pore size distribution (c, d) of B-TiO2 NCs (a, c) and W-TiO2 NCs (b, d) samples.
Ti 2p (a) and O 1s (b) core level XPS spectra for the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs. The inset of Fig. 4b is the deconvolution of O 1s peak of B-TiO NCs.
CVs at 0.2 mV s−1 (a), galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at a rate of 0.5 C (b), rate capability ranging from 0.5 C to 20 C (c) and cycling performance at 0.5 C (d) of B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs.
Nyquist plots for the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs. The inset is the equivalent circuit.
Scheme illustration for the fabrication of black TiO2 nanocrystals.
Comparison of rate capability (mAh g−1) at different rates of B-TiO2 NCs electrode with other black TiO2 electrodes reported recently.

head.ce:title: Black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals fabricated through square-wave alternating voltage as high-performance anode materials for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren, 554300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals have been successfully fabricated via electrochemical square-wave alternating voltage method. Repeated redox of Ti foil surface accompanying vigorous hydrogen evolution plays a key role on the formation of the black TiO2 nanocrystals. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area are utilized to characterize the structure and properties of the products. When utilized as anode materials for lithium-ion batteries, the black TiO2 nanocrystals electrode exhibits a high reversible capacity of 271 and 266 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (1 C = 168 mA g−1), as well as a good rate capability, which are likely to be associated with small particle size (ca. 6 nm), surface/interfacial pseudocapacitive lithium storage and increased electronic conductivity contributed from Ti3+ defects.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its intrinsic safety, low cost, long cycle life and environmental friendliness, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is attracted extensive attention in the areas of energy storage and conversion, such as secondary lithium/sodium ion battery, hybrid supercapacitor, and fuel cell [1–10]. However, the electrochemical performances of pristine TiO2 are relatively poor as a result of its poor electrical conductivity (∼10−12 S cm−1), which hinders its large-scale applications in energy-related industries [9,11].

Defect chemistry is one of the most powerful ways to tailor the properties of TiO2 [8,12–15]. In 2011, a highly conductive black TiO2 nanocrystals with abundant oxygen vacancy/Ti3+ sites and disordered surface have been designed through thermal treatment in a hydrogen atmosphere, which can greatly improve the photocatalytic activities [16]. Since then, some effective methods have been developed to fabricate black defect-engineered TiO2 with improved electrochemical, photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic properties, which include hydrogen thermal treatment, hydrogen plasma, chemical reduction and electrochemical ways [1]. Among these methods, electrochemical ways are usually carried out in mild conditions, which are convenient and controllable [17–19]. For example, a multipulse anodization method has been developed by Zheng et al. to obtain black TiO2 films [20]. These TiO2 films composed of well-defined layers displayed long-range periodicity structure and strong optical response in the visible wavelengths. Hydrogenated black TiO2 nanotubes with improved photoelectrochemical water splitting performance were fabricated by Xu et al. through a facile and eco-friendly electrochemical hydrogenation method [21]. Then, similar methods were also developed to obtain black TiO2 nanotubes photocatalysts or materials for supercapacitors [22,23]. However, these electrochemical methods for black TiO2 still require two or more electrochemical steps [20–23].

In this article, we report a one-step electrochemical square-wave alternating voltage method to fabricate black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals. The as-obtained black TiO2 nanocrystals exhibit high specific capacity, good rate capability and excellent cycling performance when utilized as anode materials for lithium ion batteries.
Ti foils and Li metal were purchased from Alfa Aesar. All other analytical grade chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Electrochemically square-wave alternating voltage method was utilized to design the black defect-engineered TiO2, which is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the Ti foil electrodes were polished with 2000 grit carbimet paper, rinsed in deionized water, and degreased with ethanol. The electrochemical cell consisted of two Ti foil electrodes with the area of 1 cm2 and 0.5 mm thick immersed in 3 M KCl. Typically, square-wave alternating voltage (at +10 V for 0.01 s, and then −10 V for 0.01 s, 50 Hz) is applied to the two Ti foils electrodes for 2 h, and then black flocculent substances start to be exfoliated from both electrodes. The black flocculent substances were then collected by centrifugation and washed completely with deionized water. Lastly, the resultant material was heat-treated at 500 °C for 2 h under Ar flow to obtain crystalline black defect-engineered TiO2 nanoparticles. For comparison, the black TiO2 nanocrystals were further heat-treated at 500 °C for 2 h under O2 flow to obtain get white TiO2 nanocrystals.

The crystallinity of the samples was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2550 VB+ 18 kW, Japan) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (0.1542 nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded by a JEM-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Surface compositions and the bonding states were examined with an ESCALab250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, BELSORP-MINIⅡ) specific surface area was obtained from the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm recorded at 77 K.

The samples dried at 80 °C for 8 h in a vacuum oven were mixed with poly(vinylidenefluoride) (PVdF) binder and acetylene black at weight ratios of 80:10:10 in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent to form a uniform slurry. Then, the resultant slurries were uniformly coated onto a Cu foil with a blade. The as-obtained electrode sheets were dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven for 12 h and pressed under a pressure of 20 MPa. CR2016-type coin cells were assembled inside an mBraun glovebox (H2O < 0.5 ppm, O2 < 0.5 ppm) using the metallic lithium counter/reference electrode, a polypropylene separator (Celgard 2400), an electrolyte of 1 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (EC/DMC, 1:1 vol). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted on CHI 660D electrochemical station (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China). Galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles were carried out with an Arbin battery cycler (BT2000) between 3.0 and 1.0 V vs Li/Li+. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured on Solartron Analytical in a two-electrode configuration at an AC voltage of 10 mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz after the 5th discharge/charge cycle.

Scheme illustration for the fabrication of black TiO2 nanocrystals was shown in Scheme 1. When the square-wave alternating voltage is applied to the Ti foils electrodes, electrochemical redox reactions and evolutions of hydrogen and oxygen occur alternately. During the anodic polarization, Ti atoms on the surface of Ti foil are oxidized to thin TiO2 films [24]. Then, the thin Ti oxides films are partially reduced to black defect-rich TiO2 when the electrode is switched to the highly cathodic polarization, accompanying vigorous hydrogen evolution [23,25]. Lastly, the black defect-rich TiO2 nanoparticles obtained through repeated redox are swept away from the Ti surface by the intensive oxygen and hydrogen evolution.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared black TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs), the black TiO2 nanocrystals (B-TiO2 NCs), the white TiO2 nanocrystals (W-TiO2 NCs), and the PDF standard card for anatase TiO2. It is clear that the black TiO2 NPs display poor crystallinity, while the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs are well crystallized, illustrating the amorphous TiO2 NPs could turned into crystals when annealed at 500 °C. The major diffraction peaks for W-TiO2 NCs and B-TiO2 NCs near at 25.1°, 36.9°, 37.8°, 38.6°, 47.9°, 53.9°, 55.0°, and 62.5° can be indexed to (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), and (213) of indexed to anatase TiO2 (PDF No. 65-5714) [26]. Interestingly, the color of B-TiO2 NCs change from black to white after 2 h following the annealing of B-TiO2 NCs under O2 atmosphere, indicating stoichiometric TiO2 regrew on the surface of defect-rich B-TiO2 NCs, similar results were also reported by Tian et al. and Stone et al. [27,28].

The morphology and microstructure of the TiO2 NPs, W-TiO2 NCs, and B-TiO2 NCs are characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As can been seen from the typical TEM images (Fig. 2 a, c and e), the particle size of these samples is about 30 nm. However, the lattice fringe for TiO2 NPs given in Fig. 2b is not clear, indicating the crystallinity of TiO2 NPs is poor, which is in agreement with the XRD results (Fig. 1). From HRTEM observation of Fig. 2d and f, it is worth noting that the TiO2 nanoparticles of both samples consist of a large number of small nanocrystals subunits (about 6 nm in size). The clear crystal planes in the subunits corresponding to the (101) planes of anatase TiO2 further confirm the formation of anatase TiO2 nanocrystals subunits.

Fig. 3 a and b show the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs, respectively. As can be seen, the two isotherms with distinct hysteresis loops at high pressures can be identified as type-IV [4]. The specific surface area of B-TiO2 NCs is calculated to be 152.3 m2 g−1, slightly higher than that of W-TiO2 NCs (141.1 m2 g−1), which is caused by the weak lattice expansion of the TiO2 nanocrystals through defect engineering [29]. The pore size distribution (Fig. 3c and d) was obtained from the isotherm adsorption branches based on Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. As can been seen, the average pore size of B-TiO2 NCs sample (16.2 nm) is much larger than that of W-TiO2 NCs sample (8.7 nm).

To examine the chemical composition and oxidation state of the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs, the surface XPS characterization was carried out. As can been seen from the Ti 2p core level XPS spectra for the W-TiO2 NCs presented in Fig. 4 a, two broad peaks centered at 459.2 and 464.9 eV correspond to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks of Ti4+ in TiO2, respectively [14]. While for the B-TiO2 NCs (Fig. 4a), a negative shift of these two peaks can be observed in the binding energy, indicating different chemical environments. These two peaks located at 458.4 and 464.0 eV, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks of Ti3+, confirm the formation of stable Ti3+ defect in the B-TiO2 NCs [30,31]. In the case of W-TiO2 NCs, stoichiometric TiO2 layers regrew on the surface of defect-rich B-TiO2 NCs when annealed in O2 atmosphere [32]. Fig. 4b compares the O 1s core level XPS spectra of B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs. The W-TiO2 NCs showed a peak of 530.1 eV corresponding to the characteristic peak of Ti-O-Ti [31]. While for the B-TiO2 NCs, not only a broader peak located at 530.1 eV but also an additional shoulder at higher binding energy can be observed. Deconvolution of O 1s peak of B-TiO2 NCs is shown in inset of Fig. 4b. The peak of 531.7 eV corresponds to Ti-OH, which is formed via binding of reductive hydrogens and defective oxygen sites on the surface of the B-TiO2 NCs [33]. These results suggest that defect-rich black TiO2 NCs were created through repeated electrochemical redox accompanied by vigorous hydrogen evolution.

EIS measurements were further carried out to study the effects of Ti3+ defects on the electrochemical performance of TiO2. Fig. 6 displays the Nyquist plots of the B-TiO2 NCs and W-TiO2 NCs electrodes after 5 cycles at 0.5 C. Both of the plots contain a semicircle followed by an inclined linear. The high frequency intercept on the Z′ axis is attributed to the internal ohmic resistance (Rs), and the semicircle in the medium-frequency range is related to the charge-transfer impedance (Rct) [37,38]. According to the modified Randles equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 6, the internal resistance of B-TiO2 NCs electrode is 1.5 Ω, which is much lower than that of W-TiO2 NCs electrode (3.4 Ω), and the charge-transfer impedance of the W-TiO2 NCs electrode (20.8 Ω) is about four times lower than that of W-TiO2 NCs electrode (77.4 Ω), indicating enhanced charge transportation due to the effect of Ti3+ defects which can greatly improve the electrical conductivity of TiO2 nanocrystals.

In conclusion, black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals have been obtained through an electrochemical square-wave alternating voltage treatment of Ti foil, which involves repeated redox of Ti foil surface accompanying vigorous hydrogen evolution. The as-fabricated black defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals utilized as anode materials for lithium-ion batteries exhibit high capacity, excellent cycling stabilities and remarkable rate capabilities. This work opens up environmentally friendly square-wave alternating voltage method to transform bulk Ti materials into colorful defect-engineered TiO2 nanocrystals for various applications such as energy storage/conversion, catalysis, sensor, and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from Department of Education of Guizhou Province (No. KY[2016]106, KY[2016]009), Department of Science and Technology of Guizhou Province (QKH[2017]1185) and Natural Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Guizhou Province (QKH [2017] 5604) are greatly appreciated.
